comments.author: cli
comments.text:
VERIFICATION FAILED: Captured logs do not contain filtering information. 

**Findings:**
- All log files (73-84 lines) contain only boot/init sequence
- Logs end at agent dispatch, before Pluck strand executes
- No bead examination records found
- No filter rule evaluation records found
- No filtering decisions captured

**Root Cause:** Capture terminated too early - logs end immediately after agent dispatch (line 73-74), before actual Pluck filtering operations occur.

**Acceptance Criteria Status:**
❌ Log file reviewed and confirmed to contain filtering information - FAILED
❌ Beads being examined are visible in logs - FAILED  
❌ Filter rules being evaluated are visible in logs - FAILED
❌ Logs are complete and not truncated - FAILED

Detailed findings documented in notes/bf-5rq6-log-verification-findings.md

- Secret contains data keys (can list the keys)
- Secret is not in failed/deleted state

Commands:
# Verify secret exists
kubectl --kubeconfig=<path> get secret armor-writer -n devimprint

# List data keys in the secret
kubectl --kubeconfig=<path> get secret armor-writer -n devimprint -o jsonpath='{.data}' | python3 -c "import json,sys; keys=json.load(sys.stdin).keys(); print('\n'.join(keys))"

title: Gate image publish on unit tests: run go vet + go test in Dockerfile build stage
description:
GAP: docs/plan/plan.md 'Testing Strategy' defines unit tests as the first line of defense, and the repo has 24 *_test.go files, but nothing runs them before an image ships. CI is the armor-build Argo WorkflowTemplate (declarative-config repo) which does a docker build + push of ronaldraygun/armor; GitHub Actions are disabled org-wide. The Dockerfile builder stage only runs 'go build' — a commit with failing unit tests still publishes an image and auto-bumps VERSION.

FIX (repo-local, no CI-template change needed): in the Dockerfile builder stage, after COPY . ., add a test gate before the binary build, e.g.:
RUN CGO_ENABLED=0 go vet ./... && CGO_ENABLED=0 go test ./...
Notes:
- tests/integration is guarded by env/skip conditions for real B2+Cloudflare (see tests/integration/README.md) — confirm those tests self-skip without credentials inside the container build; if any do not, scope the gate to ./cmd/... ./internal/... instead
- Keep the runtime stage (FROM scratch) unchanged
- Consider GOFLAGS=-buildvcs=false if the .git dir is not in build context

ACCEPTANCE:
- docker build fails when a unit test fails (verify by temporarily breaking a test locally — do not commit the breakage)
- docker build succeeds on clean HEAD with no B2/Cloudflare credentials available
- Build time increase is acceptable (<~2 min extra); document the gate in a Dockerfile comment
close_reason: Data-hygiene remediation (bf-wre): status was completed, a non-terminal Custom status invisible to is_terminal()/get_ready_candidates, silently freezing dependents. Underlying work was already done per bead history; correcting to closed.

comments.author: cli
comments.text:
All acceptance criteria verified:
1. ✅ docker build fails when a unit test fails
2. ✅ docker build succeeds on clean HEAD with no credentials
3. ✅ integration tests properly skipped (require build tags)
4. ✅ build time increase ~11s (well under 2min threshold)

Test gate implemented in commit 7a8d89d and verified.
See notes/bf-68dl.md for detailed verification results.

title: Disaster-recovery runbook: MEK backup/escrow, restore drill, rotation-failure recovery
description:
WHY: ARMOR exists because of a 2026-03-27 data-loss incident, and other infrastructure (an archive-to-B2 pipeline) is blocked on ARMOR being trustworthy as a backup target. The single most catastrophic failure mode — losing the MEK — has no documented mitigation. docs/ has cloudflare-setup.md and dashboard.md but no DR/operations doc. The admin API already provides the needed primitives (README.md 'Admin API': /admin/key/verify, /admin/key/rotate, /admin/key/export?confirm=yes; canary at /armor/canary).

WORK: write docs/disaster-recovery.md covering:
1. MEK backup/escrow procedure: exporting via /admin/key/export, where escrow copies should live conceptually (offline/secret manager — describe properties, NOT actual locations or key values), and the rule that a MEK must never be rotated without first verifying escrow of the old MEK (per-file DEKs wrapped by a lost MEK are unrecoverable)
2. Restore drill: step-by-step recovery when the ARMOR deployment/config is lost but MEK + B2 bucket survive — fresh instance with same env config, /admin/key/verify against the canary, spot-check decrypt; reference the offline decrypt CLI (bead bf-16vq) as the server-less path
3. Key-rotation failure recovery: rotation is resumable via .armor/rotation-state.json (plan.md 'Key Rotation'); document how to detect a half-rotated bucket and resume, and the implication that bucket versioning is OFF so CopyObject overwrites in place
4. Multipart caveat: objects with sidecar HMAC tables at .armor/hmac/<sha256-of-key> — recovery must preserve the .armor/ tree, never 'clean it up'
5. What is NOT recoverable: data written after last backup, objects whose wrapped DEK metadata was destroyed

ACCEPTANCE:
- docs/disaster-recovery.md exists and is linked from README.md Documentation section
- Every step names the exact endpoint/env var/file it uses; no placeholder hand-waving
- Contains no real key material, bucket names, tokens, or hostnames — generic examples only
- A restore drill was walked through at least on paper against the current code (endpoints verified to exist in internal/server)

title: Epic: ARMOR web dashboard — finalize in Go, remove Rust scaffold
description:
Supersedes umbrella bf-1mmb. Parent/tracking bead for completing the web dashboard work.

DECISION (2026-06-10): the dashboard direction is Go, not Rust.
Evidence:
- The entire ARMOR core is Go (go.mod, cmd/armor, internal/*)
- The dashboard is ALREADY substantially implemented in Go: internal/dashboard/dashboard.go (~1217 lines) + dashboard_test.go (~1354 lines), wired into internal/server/server.go AdminHandler() at /dashboard/* on ARMOR_ADMIN_LISTEN (default 127.0.0.1:9001)
- docs/plan/plan.md Phase 3 marks the web dashboard COMPLETE (~line 724); docs/dashboard.md documents it; dashboard auth (bf-1f7b) and key-rotation UI (bf-2yba) are done and closed
- The Rust/Actix-web scaffold (src/main.rs, Cargo.toml, Cargo.lock; commits 3609ad2, a5e96b5, 931684d) was produced by a misdirected worker session that assumed ARMOR was a new Rust project. It duplicates a trivial subset (health endpoint) of the Go server and is abandoned.

REMAINING WORK (children, in dependency order):
1. bf-2928 — remove the abandoned Rust/Actix scaffold
2. bf-4ox4 — JSON object-listing API endpoint (the one genuine feature gap)
3. bf-1daa — verify bucket browser UI acceptance criteria, fill test gaps
4. bf-668r — verify encryption status + cache statistics display, fill gaps

CLOSE WHEN: all four children are closed, go build/test green, docs/dashboard.md + docs/plan/plan.md consistent with final state. Record child completions as comments on this bead as they close.

History: bf-449o (web server framework, with split-children bf-4rm7/bf-5zxa/bf-tojg/bf-tr44) closed 2026-06-10 as already-satisfied by the existing Go server. bf-2c2x/bf-1q8r/bf-16zh/bf-2f8b (create-Rust-project chain) closed as obsolete. Build verified green 2026-06-10: go build ./... and go test ./... both pass.

description:

## Verification Commands
1. Check git status (should show no credential files):
git status --porcelain | grep -E 'litestream.*key' || echo 'No credential files in git - good'

2. Verify both files exist with secure permissions:
ls -la /tmp/litestream_access_key_id.txt /tmp/litestream_secret_key_decoded.txt

3. Verify files contain data (check non-empty and not error messages):
head -c 20 /tmp/litestream_access_key_id.txt
head -c 20 /tmp/litestream_secret_key_decoded.txt

## Success Criteria
- Both files exist
- Both have secure permissions (600)
- Neither file is tracked by git
- Both files contain valid credential data (not error messages)

title: Decode and validate LITESTREAM_ACCESS_KEY_ID
description:
## Child Bead: Decode and validate

This bead decodes the base64 value and validates it's a proper AWS access key.

### Prerequisites
- Previous child bead complete (base64 value retrieved)

### Acceptance Criteria
- Successfully decoded the base64 value to plain text
- Decoded value is not empty
- Value appears valid (starts with AKIA... or similar AWS access key pattern)
- Value is human-readable (not corrupted/garbled)

### Commands
```bash
# Decode the value
base64 -d /tmp/litestream_key_id.b64 > /tmp/litestream_key_id.txt

# Display and validate the value
cat /tmp/litestream_key_id.txt

# Verify it looks like an AWS access key (starts with AKIA and is 20 chars)
grep -q '^AKIA[0-9A-Z]{16}$' /tmp/litestream_key_id.txt && echo "Valid AWS access key format"
```

Success: Decoded value is displayed, is non-empty, and matches AWS access key format.

comments.author: cli
comments.text:
PREREQUISITE NOT MET: This bead cannot complete because the previous child bead did not successfully retrieve the base64 value. 

The file /tmp/litestream_key_id.b64 contains an RBAC error message documenting that the kubectl-proxy for ord-devimprint has read-only RBAC that blocks secret access.

Error: User 'system:serviceaccount:devpod-observer:devpod-observer' cannot get resource 'secrets' in namespace 'devimprint'.

This task requires a valid base64-encoded AWS access key to decode and validate, but no actual base64 value was retrieved by the prior child bead.

comments.author: cli
comments.text: Verified RBAC blocker still present - /tmp/litestream_key_id.b64 contains error message, not base64 data. Decode failed with 'invalid input'. Task cannot complete until prerequisite bead successfully retrieves the base64 value (requires RBAC resolution).

title: Hygiene sweep: purge tracked artifacts, dead CI workflows, doc drift
description:
Automated hygiene sweep (2026-07-11 corpus-audit follow-up). Findings: run the checker for details.

Steps:
1. git pull origin (merge only; NEVER force-push).
2. Run the hygiene checker: script repo_hygiene.sh with --json and this repo as argument. It ships in the jeds-curated-skills repo (checkout in the home directory), folder repo-hygiene, subfolder scripts. Full fix rules: SKILL.md in that same folder.
3. Apply ONLY these fixes, ONE COMMIT PER CATEGORY:
   a) add missing .gitignore entries (build dirs, caches)
   b) git rm --cached all tracked build artifacts and binaries (keep files on disk)
   c) delete tracked GitHub Actions workflow files (Actions are disabled estate-wide; CI is Argo Workflows)
   d) fix README version-badge and CI-badge drift
4. FORBIDDEN: touching source code, git stash, git clean, git reset, no-verify commits, force-push. The dirty-working-tree and stash-pileup findings are REPORT-ONLY context - do not act on them.
5. If a pre-commit hook rejects a commit (provenance/fixture hooks), leave that category unfixed and record the hook output in a comment.
6. Re-run the checker. Acceptance: tracked build artifacts = 0, dead workflow files = 0, gitignore gaps = 0 (or explicitly noted hook-blocked). Push to origin and include the final checker summary in the close comment.
